coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots embedded Co3O4 with multiwall carbon nanotubes: An efficient probe for the simultaneous determination of anticancer and antibiotic drugs
coredata.dc:creator: Muthusankar, Ganesan
Devi, Ramadhass Keerthika
Gopu, Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: The simultaneous determination of anticancer and antibiotic drugs in biological samples with fast and sensitive methods is an essential task for the effective monitoring of drug therapy. A novel electrochemical sensor for the simultaneous determination of anticancer drug Flutamide (FLU) and antibiotic drug Nitrofurantoin (NF) was developed based on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNTs hybrid nanocomposite. The electro-kinetics of the developed sensor was studied using cyclic voltammetry (CV). The electrochemical performance of FLU and NF on the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNTs/GCE surface was examined using CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques. At an optimized condition, the developed sensor exhibits excellent performance in simultaneous determination of FLU and NF with a linear range of 0.05–590 μM and 0.05–1220 μM and detection limit of 0.0169 μM and 0.044 μM respectively. Furthermore, the developed sensor exhibits satisfactory results in the real sample analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anticancer drug
Antibiotic drug
Simultaneous determination
Carbon quantum dot
Co3O4

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite modified electrode
Electrochemical studies of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite modified electrode
Electrochemical behavior of FLU and NF at N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE
Simultaneous determination of FLU and NF

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge to UGC – BSR ( 25-1/2014 – 2015(BSR) / 7–88/2007(BSR) , Dt.:15th-Oct-2015) fellowship for financial assistance. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support given by the RUSA-Phase 2.0 grant (vide Letter No. F. 24-51/2014-U (TNMulti-Gen), Department of Education, Govt. of India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite (b) FT-IR spectra of N-CQD, Co3O4, and N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite, HR-TEM images of (c) N-CQD (Insert: SAED pattern) (d & e) N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite at different magnifications, (g–i) N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite at different magnifications, and (f) SAED pattern of N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite.

CV responses of different modified electrodes in (a) 1 mM of [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.1 M KCl solution at 50 mV s−1 (b) 100 μM FLU and 100 μM NF in the phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (c) CVs of different scan rate (20 – 200 mV s−1) at the surface of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE with the presence of FLU and NF (200 μM each) in the phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) (d) linear plot of reduction peak (R1) current vs. scan rate for FLU and NF (e) CVs of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE at different pH (3–11) of phosphate buffer and (f) calibration plot for pH vs. Epa and Ipa.

(a) DPV responses obtained at N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE for the successive addition of FLU (0.05–590 μM) in the presence of 600 μM NF (b) The calibration plot for the cathodic peak current vs. FLU concentration and the maximum relative standard deviation (RSD) reaches to 2.12% (c) DPV responses of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE for the successive addition of NF (0.05–1220 μM) in the presence of 400 μM FLU (d) the plot between the cathodic peak current vs. NF concentrations and the maximum RSD value is 2.41% (e) DPV response at N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE for the simultaneous additions of FLU and NF from 4 to 850 μM in phosphate buffer solution (pH 7) and (f) the calibration plot for the cathodic peak current of FLU and NF vs. concentrations of FLU and NF and the maximum RSD value is 2.63%.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of hybrid nanocomposite and its application towards simultaneous electrochemical determination of Flutamide and Nitrofurantoin.
The overall redox mechanism of FLU and NF on N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots embedded Co3O4 with multiwall carbon nanotubes: An efficient probe for the simultaneous determination of anticancer and antibiotic drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, 630003, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, 630003, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The simultaneous determination of anticancer and antibiotic drugs in biological samples with fast and sensitive methods is an essential task for the effective monitoring of drug therapy. A novel electrochemical sensor for the simultaneous determination of anticancer drug Flutamide (FLU) and antibiotic drug Nitrofurantoin (NF) was developed based on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNTs hybrid nanocomposite. The electro-kinetics of the developed sensor was studied using cyclic voltammetry (CV). The electrochemical performance of FLU and NF on the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNTs/GCE surface was examined using CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques. At an optimized condition, the developed sensor exhibits excellent performance in simultaneous determination of FLU and NF with a linear range of 0.05–590 μM and 0.05–1220 μM and detection limit of 0.0169 μM and 0.044 μM respectively. Furthermore, the developed sensor exhibits satisfactory results in the real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroaromatic compounds are widely used industrial chemicals and have been utilized to synthesize biologically essential drugs, including anti-cancer, anti-biotic, antitubercular drugs, etc. Flutamide (FLU) and nitrofurantoin (NF) are nitro groups containing anti-cancer and anti-biotic drugs, respectively. FLU is a non-steroidal anti-androgen drug. It has been prescribed to treat prostate cancer in men and to treat polycystic ovarian syndrome (excess of androgen level) in women (Švorc et al., 2017). NF is an antibacterial agent for urinary tract infections. Specifically, NF has prescribed to pregnant woman to treat the infections in urinary tract. However, the overdosage of these drugs brings side effects, such as blood in urine, inflamed prostate, drowsiness, nausea, methemoglobinemia, and liver malfunctions (He and Li, 2019a; Møller et al., 1990; Vinoth Kumar et al., 2018).

Moreover, the critical side effect of NF is to produce birth defects in embryo or fetus (Lee et al., 2008). Further, the poor metabolism of FLU & NF, the metabolites are evacuated through urine, and feces (Švorc et al., 2017). In addition to that, the presence of the high electron-withdrawing nitro group in FLU and NF, caused the poor biodegradation and makes the environment at risk (Chen et al., 2012). Therefore, in this work we have chosen the FLU and NF as a model-analyte for simultaneous quantification.

The analytical techniques including mass spectroscopy (Khan et al., 2015), fluorescence (Wang et al., 2018), chromatography (Belal, 1988; Carvalho et al., 2014) and electrochemistry (Balasubramanian et al., 2019; Kokulnathan et al., 2019) have been developed for the individual detection and quantification of FLU and NF. However, the simultaneous determination of FLU and NF is reported here for the first time. Among the various analytical techniques, electrochemical techniques are highly favorable due to the advantages of its high sensitivity, low-cost, and simple instrumentation with user-friendly. However, the redox peak of the model drugs tends to overlap at the conventional glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and cannot be distinguished. Thus, the innovative modified electrode is required for effective simultaneous determination and it also a key factor to solve the practical difficulties.

In recent decades, nanostructured transition metal oxides have been used as an electrode modifier for electrochemical sensing applications (Wang et al., 2017). Among theme cobalt oxide (Co3O4) nanostructures are promising electrode material due to the fact of its electrochemical stability and having a higher surface area to volume ratio (Qiu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). Further to improve the charge transfer between electrode and electrolyte interface, Co3O4 has been hybridized with different carbon nanomaterials such as graphene, nanotubes, graphene oxides (Haldorai et al., 2016; Qiu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). Recently our research group has developed the electrode materials based on metal oxides loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) to enhance the charge transfer ratio between electrode/electrolyte interface (G. Muthusankar et al., 2019; Ganesan Muthusankar et al., 2019). Furthermore, having the graphitic π conjugated structure of N-CQDs offers to anchor with other carbon nanostructures to result the functional hybrid materials. Thus, it is expected that the incorporation of N-CQDs in the nanostructure of Co3O4 and its further hybridization with multiwall carbon nanotube will result a promising electrocatalyst for the simultaneous determination of drug molecules.

In the present work, we have synthesized the N-CQDs loaded Co3O4 (N-CQD@Co3O4) nanocomposite through a rapid and straightforward microwave synthesis approach. Then the nanocomposite was hybridized with MWCNTs using the ultrasonication method. The N-CQDs in the nanocomposite interact with the hydrophobic core of MWCNTs and forms a stable N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite. The presence of MWCNTs exhibits high stability and high mechanical strength to the hybrid nanocomposite. This hybrid nanocomposite was used as an electrode modifier, and its simultaneous sensing behavior is reported here.
The materials & reagents, synthesis of N-CQDs, and characterization methods are given in the supporting information.

1.455 g of Co(NO3)2.6H2O and 0.83 g of CTAB were dissolved in 25 mL of distilled water separately. The solutions were mixed with a dropwise addition under stirring condition. To the mixture solution, 5 mL of N-CQD was added and stirred well. Then the reaction mixture was exposed with microwave irradiation for 10 min using a domestic microwave oven. The resulted product was cooled at room temperature, then washed thrice with water and ethanol. The obtained precipitated was dried at 80 °C overnight and then calcinated at 500 °C for 4 h. Finally, the N-CQD decorated cobalt oxide nanoparticles were obtained. A similar procedure was used for the synthesis of pure cobalt oxide except N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mg of N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite was added in isopropanol (1 mL) and dispersed well-using ultrasonication (optimization of material concentration is given in the supporting information). Then 5 mg of MWCNT was added to the above dispersion and subjected to the ultrasonic irradiation for about 30 min. A black colored suspension was resulted, from which an optimized concentration (5 μL) was pipetted out and coated on the polished glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. Then it was dried at room temperature to get the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite modified GCE (N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE). The synthesis and fabrication procedures are displayed in Scheme 1 .

Electrochemical studies were performed using the Autolab PGSTATE 30 instrument. A conventional three-electrode system having a GCE as a working electrode, saturated Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, and platinum as a counter electrode were utilized for all electrochemical investigations. The electrochemical performance of the modified electrodes was analyzed using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. The measurements were carried out in a three-electrode cell assembly, which contains 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe in 0.1 M KCl solution. The CV technique was carried out between the potentials −0.4 and 0.8 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The EIS was recorded under open circuit potential in a frequency range from 100 mHz to 100 kHz with 50 mV amplitude. The electrochemical responses of the analytes on the surface of modified electrodes were examined using CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH 7.0) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. CVs were recorded under an optimized potential window of 0.4 to −0.8 V. Whereas, the DPV was performed by scanning the potential towards the negative side from 0 to −0.8 V with the step potential of 10 mV and the modulation amplitude of 50 mV.

Fig. S2a demonstrates the broad XRD pattern of N-CQDs centered at 28°, which suggests its amorphous nature. The XRD pattern of the N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 1 a. The observed diffraction patterns at (111), (220), (311), (400), (511), (440), and (620) planes were attributed to the cubic phase of Co3O4 (JCPDS # 00-009-0418) with Fd-3m space group and confirms the crystallinity of the material. Furthermore, the absence of a broad peak around 23° suggests that the minimum percentage of N-CQDs present in the nanocomposite. The average grain size of the nanocomposite was calculated using Debye-Scherrer equation, which was 42.86 nm. FT-IR spectra of N-CQD, pristine Co3O4, and N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 1b. N-CQDs shows the peaks at 3323, 2126, 1627 and 705 cm−1 which corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH, NCN (Tan et al., 2016), CC & CN, and –CH (alkyl) groups (Singh et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2014) respectively. The pristine Co3O4 exhibits two sharp peaks at 562 and 667 cm−1, which are due to the metal oxide stretching vibrations of Co3+ - O bond and Co2+ - O bond of the spinel structure (Liu et al., 2017) respectively. However, the peak intensity of Co2+ - O bond is decreased for N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite and shifted from 667 to 675 cm−1. Thus, it suggests that the interaction exists between the N-CQD and Co3O4 and it caused a decrease in peak intensity.

The surface morphology of the materials was characterized by SEM, and its results are discussed in the supporting information section S3.1. Fig. 1 exhibits the HR-TEM and SAED pattern of the N-CQD and N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite. Fig. 1c shows the uniform distribution of N-CQDs, and it has the size ranged between 7 and 9 nm. The inset figure in Fig. 1c displays SAED pattern, which suggests the amorphous nature of the N-CQDs. Furthermore, the EDX spectrum of N-CQDs confirms the presence of N, C, and O in its structure (Fig. S2b). The HR-TEM images of N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite confirm the nanosized structure with average particle size of 32 nm. The observed particle size is near to the calculated value from the XRD results. Moreover, the bright lattice fringes depict the inter-planar spacing of 0.42 nm (Fig. 1e), which corresponds to the (311) plane of Co3O4. The SAED pattern (Fig. 1f) exhibits a distinct ring pattern, suggesting the crystalline nature of Co3O4. Further a black dot found to be scattered over the surface confirms the presence of N-CQD in the nanocomposite. Fig. 1g–i shows the TEM images of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite at different magnifications, in which the integration of N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite on to the sidewalls of MWCNTs was clearly provided. The successful integration is due to the presence of N-CQD in an N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite, which hydrophobically interacts with MWCNTs through its graphitic π conjugated structure. This interaction leads to attach with number of MWCNTs (Fig. 1h) and forms a sandwich-like hybrid nanocomposite material. The TEM results clearly evident that the interconnection between N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite and MWCNT. Moreover, it also suggests that the followed simple ultrasonication method is effectful for the successful preparation of hybrid nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical studies of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The electron transfer kinetics of the modified electrodes were examined in CV and EIS techniques, with a ferro/ferricyanide redox system as a probe. Fig. 2 a shows the CVs of different modified electrodes. The anodic and cathodic peak-to-peak separation value (ΔE
p
= E
pa
- E
pc ) on bare GCE, Co3O4/GCE, N-CQD/GCE, N-CQD@Co3O4/GCE, MWCNT/GCE and N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE are found to be 97, 93, 85, 82, 79 and 76 mV respectively. The results suggest the reversibility of the electrode process is steadily increased from bare GCE to N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE. This enhanced electron transfer rate with the increased current response and reduced ΔE
p value suggest that the electron transport and electrochemical surface area (A
eff ) are increased in each modification. As a result, enhanced electrode kinetics is resulted in N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE than the other modified electrodes. The A
eff of the modified electrodes is calculated using Randles-Sevick equation (Bard and Faulkner, 2002).
(1)
I
pa = (2.687 × 105) n
3/2
v
1/2
D
1/2
A
eff
C°
Where D (0.76 × 10−5 cm2 s−1) is the diffusion coefficient of the ferricyanide system (Pathak et al., 2019). I
pa , C°, A
eff , v, and n refers to the anodic peak current, the concentration of the probe, electrochemical surface area, scan rate, and a number of electrons transfer respectively. Accordingly, the active surface area of bare GCE, Co3O4/GCE, N-CQD/GCE, N-CQD@Co3O4/GCE, MWCNT/GCE, and N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE are calculated to be 0.064 cm2, 0.069 cm2, 0.072, 0.076 cm2, 0.079, and 0.089 cm2, respectively.

Fig. S4 shows the EIS results in the form of the Nyquist plot with the corresponding Randles circuit. Form the results the charge transfer resistance (R ct ) of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE was measured to be 2.75 Ω. Whereas, the R ct value of N-CQD@Co3O4/GCE, MWCNT/GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Co3O4/GCE, and bare GCE were measured to be 2.03, 8.03, 27.70, 63.46, and 140.94 Ω respectively. As a result, the modification with N-CQD and MWCNT highly reduces the R ct value in hybrid nanocomposite. Thus, the results reveal that N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE can provide good electrical contact between the electrode and supporting electrolyte interface and made the charge transfer easier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of FLU and NF at N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic behavior of modified and unmodified GCE was examined by CV technique in the PBS (pH 7.0) containing 100 μM FLU and 100 μM NF at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1. It can be seen from Fig. 2b, bare GCE exhibited a weak reduction peak with low peak currents for FLU and NF, suggesting the very slow direct electron transfer at the surface of bare GCE. When the GCE was modified with Co3O4, a slightly improved current response resulted for FLU and NF with distinct peaks. The N-CQD modified GCE showed sharp redox peaks with minimum peak currents for FLU and NF. Besides, N-CQD@Co3O4 presented a well-marked sharp redox peak with enhanced peak currents for FLU and NF, signifying that the higher electron transfer efficiency of N-CQD@Co3O4. This is due to the presence of N-CQD, which separates the generated charges at the interface and offers a better charge transfer pathway to the modified electrode (Nekoueian et al., 2019). Likewise, MWCNT also offers good electron transport, with distinct redox peaks due to its higher surface area. Conversely, at the electrode surface of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE, the substantial increase is observed in redox peak current intensity of the FLU and NF. This excellent behavior of the electrode material can be attributed to the synergistic involvement between N-CQD@Co3O4 and MWCNTs, which tremendously increase the electrochemical activity.

The bare GCE, Co3O4/GCE, and N-CQD/GCE exhibit the reduction peak of FLU (R1-FLU) at −0.65, −0.62, and −0.53 V with low peak current value of −20, −38, and −41 μA (Fig. 2b). Besides, both MWCNT/GCE and N-CQD@Co3O4/GCE show the reduction at same potential of −0.53 V with a similar current intensity of −77 and −74 μA respectively. Whereas N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE shows a very sharp reduction peak (R1-FLU) at −0.53 V with highest peak current of −128 μA. On the other hand, the reveres scan at N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE exhibits the cathodic (R2-FLU) and anodic (O1 -FLU) peak at −0.06 and −0.004 V, respectively. For NF, the electrodes bare GCE, Co3O4/GCE, N-CQD/GCE, N-CQD@Co3O4/GCE, and MWCNT/GCE show the reduction peak (R1 - NF) at −0.46, −0.44, −0.40, −0.39, and −0.38 V with peak current values −11.6, −19.10, −40.96, −64.04, and −79.20 μA respectively. Besides, N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE demonstrates the well-marked sharp reduction peak (R1-NF) at −0.38 V with an enhanced peak current response of −149.69 μA. Furthermore, the oxidation peak (O1 - NF) was observed at 0.17 V during the reverse scan.

In general, the nitro functional group determines the electrochemical redox mechanism of nitroaromatics. Initially, the nitro group (NO2) is reduced to hydroxylamine (R1) by a 2H-/2e-process. Then hydroxylamine undergoes the reversible (oxidation (O1)/reduction (R2)) conversion to result the nitroso compound by 4H-/4e-process (Scheme 2 ) (Kubendhiran et al., 2018; Laviron et al., 1994) The similar electro reduction mechanism was observed for FLU and NF at the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE. Though FLU and NF having the nitro groups in its structure, the reduction potential was varied, R1-FLU at - 0.53 V and R1-NF at - 0.38 V. This is due to the influence of the electron-withdrawing group and electron-donating group present in the FLU and NF respectively. The presence of the electron-withdrawing group (CF3) in FLU attracts the electrons toward itself through inductive effect, which makes the NO2 group electron deficient. Thus, the protonation (reduction) requires higher overpotential (- 0.53 V). Whereas, the electron donation and conjugation present in the structure of NF, which donates the electrons or the nitro group withdraw the electrons towards itself inductively and it is ready to protonate (reduction). So, the reduction occurs at low overpotential (−0.38 V) than FLU. Thus, the well-separated peaks were observed for the simultaneous determination of FLU and NF.

The electrochemical behavior of FLU and NF with the effect of pH at the surface of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE was performed. Fig. 2e shows the CVs of FLU and NF (150 μM each) in different pH solutions range from 3.0 to 11.0. The increasing pH causes the shift of cathodic peak potential towards the more negative potential side. Moreover, at the decreasing pH, cathodic peak potential was shifted to the less negative potential side, indicates the participation of hydrogen ions in the reduction of FLU and NF on the surface of the modified electrode. However, pH 7.0 shows the maximum redox peak current for FLU and NF (Fig. 2f). Thus, it was chosen as an optimum pH for further electrochemical experiments. The observation was well aligned with the proposed mechanism. Further, the linear relationship with correlation coefficient between the reduction potentials of analytes and pH can be described as follows:
Epa (VFLU) = −0.038 pH – 0.278, R2 = 0.9963
Epa (VNF) = −0.0405 pH – 0.116, R2 = 0.9961

The obtained slope values from the linear plot (Fig. S5) indicate the involvement of four-electron and two protons in the electrocatalytic reduction of FLU and NF (He and Li, 2019b; Karthik et al., 2017; Rezaeifar et al., 2018).

The effect of scan rate towards the redox process of FLU and NF on the modified electrode surface was studied. Fig. 2c Shows the CV responses of the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE in PBS (pH 7) with the presence of FLU and NF (200 μM each) at different scan rates ranging from 20 to 200 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. x, the cathodic peak intensities were continuously increased upon increasing the scan rate, and the peak potential was slightly shifted to the negative side. The relationship between the cathodic peak current of the FLU and NF with the increasing scan rate is shown in Fig. 2d. Good linearity was observed for the scan rate vs. current and the corresponding linear regression equation of FLU and NF can be expressed as follows,
Ipc (μA) for FLU = −1.0972x – 31.07 (R2 = 0.9944)
Ipc (μA) for NF = −1.0821x – 23.76 (R2 = 0.9968)

As a result, it was concluded that the electrochemical redox process of FLU and NF are adsorption-controlled on the surface of N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT modified GCE (Karthik et al., 2017; Lacasse et al., 1993).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of FLU and NF
ce:section.ce:para: Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) is a more sensitive analytical technique than CV for the simultaneous determination of the analytes. Thus, we have performed DPV for the simultaneous determination of FLU and NF at the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT modified GCE surface in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7). Fig. 3 a depicts the DPV profiles of various concentrations (0.05–590 μM) of FLU in the presence of 600 μM NF at modified electrode. Upon each addition of FLU, the cathodic peak current was increased, and the cathodic peak current of NF was remaining unchanged. A linear plot was obtained between peak current response and the increasing concentration of FLU, as shown in Fig. 3b. The linear relationship can be expressed in the equation of I = −0.1977 μA μM−1 – 41.355 (R2 = 0.9939). From the calibration plot, the detection limit of about 0.0169 μM with a sensitivity of 2.25 μA μM−1 cm−2 was found for the determination of FLU and the maximum relative standard deviation (RSD) reaches to 2.12%. The electrochemical determination of NF was performed in the co-existence of 400 μM FLU with the increasing concentration (0.05–1220 μM) of NF, and its DPV profiles were shown in Fig. 3c. It can be observed that the cathodic peak of NF has resulted at −0.40 V, and the peak current intensity increased with increasing of NF concentration. However, the cathodic peak current responses of FLU were slightly decreased upon the addition of NF. The linearity between the peak current response and the increasing concentration of NF is displayed in Fig. 3d. The linear regression equation I = −0.0748x – 18.174 (R2 = 0.9966) was obtained for the determination of NF. In addition to that the developed modified electrode also exhibited the wide linear range from 0.05 to 1220 μM, and the limit of detection of 0.044 μM with good sensitivity of 9.591 μA μM−1 cm−2 for the determination of NF with the RSD value of 2.41%. The obtained analytical parameters of the present work were compared with previous sensing protocols and tabulated in Tables S1 and S2.

The above experimental conditions were maintained for the simultaneous addition of FLU and NF. The equal concentration (4 – 850 μM) of FLU and NF was added, and the obtained results are displayed in Fig. 3e. Interestingly, well separated cathodic peaks of FLU and NF were obtained, and its peak current responses were increased with each addition. Further, a plot between peak current response and the analytes indicates the linear relationship (Fig. 3f), and it can be expressed in an equation of I = −0.1508x – 29.792 (R2 = 0.9909) for FLU and I = −0.093x – 16.1652 (R2 = 0.99) for NF respectively. These results explore that the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite is an excellent electrode modifier for the sensitive determination of anti-cancer drug FLU and anti-biotic drug NF. The synergistic effect between the individual constituent of the composite material has responsible for the higher electrocatalytic behavior of the modified electrode.

The selectivity is an essential parameter for the sensor, which was examined by DPV in the presence of structurally relevant interfering molecules. Fig. S6 shows the recorded DPV signals using N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE for the response of 100 μM of FLU and NF in the presence of 25 fold excess concentration of nilutamide (NLT), 4-nitroaniline (4-NA), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 4-nitrobenzene (4-NB), chloramphenicol (CAP), metronidazole (MTZ) and 50 fold excess concentration of K+, Cu2+, and Br− ion. The maximum concentration of the interfering molecules was defined from the tolerance limit, which caused nearly 5% relative error for the simultaneous determination of FLU and NF. There is no shift in the reduction peak potential, but a negligible amount of change in peak current response in the presence of interfering substances is observed (Figs. S6a and b). Thus, the results suggest that the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite can exhibit decent selectivity towards the recognition of FLU and NF in an aqueous solution. Furthermore, the sensor was investigated for the reproducibility. For this purpose, parallel DPV measurements were performed for the determination of FLU and NF using five independent N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 2.4% and 3.1% respectively. Besides, the sensor was stable up to 3 weeks for the simultaneous determination of FLU and NF. The observed results depict that only 4.8% and 6.3% of the current value was decreased from the initial current response of FLU and NF, respectively. Thus, the results reveal that the N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite can act as an excellent electrode modifier for the simultaneous determination of FLU and NF with satisfactory performance in selectivity, reproducibility, and stability.

The practical applicability of the proposed sensor was evaluated in the real samples. A known concentration of FLU and NF were spiked into the human urine sample, which was collected from the healthy volunteer. The spiked samples were diluted with PBS (pH 7) in a 1:100 ratio. Then the analysis was performed with similar experimental conditions as of sections 2.4 & 3.7 using N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT/GCE in the diluted spiked urine sample. The concentration of the FLU and NF were determined using the standard addition method, and the analytical results are tabulated in Table S1. Fig. S7 shows the DPV signals of FLU and NF from the real samples. The sensor exhibits good recovery results in the spiked urine samples. Thus, the developed sensor demonstrates its suitability for real-time usages.

In summary, a simple microwave method was used for the synthesis of N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite. The presence of N-CQD promotes the electrocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite. Further, the N-CQD@Co3O4 nanocomposite was hybridized with MWCNTs using the ultrasonication method. The graphitic π conjugated structure of N-CQDs offers to anchor with MWCNTs to construct the hybrid nanocomposite. N-CQD@Co3O4/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite has been utilized as an electrode modifier for the simultaneous determination of FLU and NF for the first time. The electrochemical behavior of FLU and NF on the surface of the proposed sensor by CV and DPV techniques evidenced that the hybrid nanocomposite offers a large number of reactive sites for the redox reaction of FLU and NF. The synergetic effect between the N-CQD, Co3O4, and MWCNT brings the high conductivity and high surface area to the hybrid nanocomposite. Interestingly, the proposed sensor exhibits wide linear range of 0.05–590 μM for FLU and 0.05–1220 μM for NF with detection limit of 0.0169 μM and 0.044 μM respectively. Furthermore, the practical applicability of the proposed sensor was examined in human urine samples. Thus, the present work exhibits the simple way to synthesis hybrid nanocomposite and its application towards the simultaneous determination of drug molecules.

Ganesan Muthusankar: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Ramadhass Keerthika Devi: Formal analysis, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Gopalakrishnan Gopu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing - original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge to UGC – BSR (25-1/2014 – 2015(BSR)/7–88/2007(BSR), Dt.:15th-Oct-2015) fellowship for financial assistance. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support given by the RUSA-Phase 2.0 grant (vide Letter No. F. 24-51/2014-U (TNMulti-Gen), Department of Education, Govt. of India.

The authors are also thankful to Dr. N. Muthuchamy, Post-Doctoral Fellow, Shenzhen University, China, and Dr. N. Sengottuvelan, Assistant Professor & Dr. S. Muthu Prabhu, Senior Post-Doctoral Fellow, Department of Industrial Chemistry, Tamil Nadu, India for their suggestions and scientific discussions.
